critique.summary: The article provides an update on the relationship between OpenTTD and Atari's re-release of Transport Tycoon Deluxe (TTD), explaining that OpenTTD remains available for free but new players on Steam and GOG must first purchase TTD. The OpenTTD team emphasizes collaboration with Atari and the importance of balancing commercial interests with community access.
mainPoints: Atari approached OpenTTD to discuss their plans for the Transport Tycoon Deluxe re-release and its impact on OpenTTD.
OpenTTD is not being pressured by Atari but has agreed to a compromise requiring new players on Steam and GOG to purchase TTD before accessing OpenTTD.
OpenTTD remains free and available for download from its official website.
Removing OpenTTD entirely from Steam and GOG was rejected to avoid disrupting current players and limiting new player discovery.
Atari has agreed to contribute financially to OpenTTD's server costs as part of the collaboration.
The OpenTTD project retains full independence despite the collaboration with Atari.
critique.truthfulness: The claims in the article align with publicly available information about the recent re-release of Transport Tycoon Deluxe by Atari and the impact on OpenTTD's availability on digital platforms. The collaboration and compromise described are consistent with statements from the OpenTTD project and Atari, as reported in official communications. The article does not present unsupported or false claims and provides a clear explanation of the situation from the OpenTTD perspective.
considerations: The article does not provide perspectives from Atari beyond the summary of their intentions, so the full scope of Atari's commercial strategy is not detailed.
Community reactions and criticisms are acknowledged but not deeply explored, which may be relevant for understanding the broader impact.
The article does not discuss potential long-term implications for OpenTTD's development or community engagement beyond the immediate changes.
Details about how the purchase requirement is technically enforced on Steam and GOG are not explained.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers on the recent changes to OpenTTD's distribution on Steam and GOG, highlighting the balance between open-source preservation and commercial interests of Atari. Commenters debate the legitimacy of Atari's role, the impact on the community, and the broader implications for software licensing and platform control.

comments.author: aeturnum
comments.text: I don't have much to add except to say that I think this is a stand-out example of how companies and preservationists should work together and not against each other. The childish folks who are upset about this aren't familiar with the realties of either open source games perseveration nor the realities of being an IP holder. This is as close as we have gotten to the Good Place. I wish Atari luck on the re-release and I hope that anyone who's upset about it reflects on why they are upset.
comments.analysis: This comment accurately captures the cooperative nature of the resolution and highlights the complexities of IP and open-source preservation.

comments.author: legitster
comments.text:
As a sidenote, this whole situation implies just how important platforms are.

Nothing about OpenTTD has changed. You can literally just go download it off their website for free - same as it was 20 years ago. And you can add it to your Steam library just fine. It's only been on the Steam store for 5 of those years.

But the open internet is dead now and just being "de-merchandised" from a platform feels like being relegated to the dark web (maybe something the open source community doesn't quite fully appreciate).
comments.analysis: The comment correctly points out that the open-source version remains freely available, emphasizing the importance of platform control in distribution.

comments.author: CivBase
comments.text:
> we have not been “pressured” by Atari to make these changes.

> Atari approached us to explain their plans for the Transport Tycoon Deluxe re-release, and what it might mean for OpenTTD.

> we understood that a compromise would be needed to balance Atari’s commercial interests […] against the availability of a free, well-developed evolution of the game.

Sounds to me like you were pressured by Atari to make these changes.
comments.analysis: This comment interprets the official statement as indirect pressure from Atari, a reasonable inference given the context.

comments.author: mikkupikku
comments.text: Seems reasonable to me. Back when I started playing OpenTTD, about 20 years ago, you had to provide your own data files from your ostensibly legal copy of TTD. They changed that after they started distributing free alternative graphics, but to be frank the strict legal status of both OpenTTD and OpenRCT2 has always seemed mildly dubious to me, on account of both projects being based off disassembled code. Atari is being fairly reasonable and gentlemenly about this.
comments.analysis: This comment provides useful legal context about the projects' origins, which is relevant to understanding Atari's commercial interests.

comments.author: paxys
comments.text:
I'm sure I'm missing some context but what is Atari's role here exactly? Isn't OpenTTD an independent and fully legal project? What is Atari's basis for asking for a "compromise"?

Or is it just the case that the project maintainers got paid off?
comments.analysis: Raises a valid question about Atari's role, reflecting common community confusion about IP rights versus open-source development.

comments.text:
This is beyond reasonable.

You can still download it for free outside of Steam.

If I make a Sonic fan game and Sega is like, you can keep it online, but just not on Steam, that’s nice.

In this situation you still have the option of playing it on Steam for a modest price

The alternative is the Nintendo route…
comments.analysis: This comment draws a reasonable analogy to other IP holders' practices, supporting the idea that restricting platform distribution is common.

comments.author: Lammy
comments.text:
> a compromise would be needed to balance Atari’s commercial interests (which of course they are entitled to pursue as the rights holder)

No, fuck 'em. They had nothing to do with developing the game, and in a sane copyright structure a thirty-year-old work would be public domain by now.
comments.analysis: While expressing a popular sentiment, this comment oversimplifies copyright law and ignores current legal realities.

comments.author: junaru
comments.text: Atari is releasing an inferior product and needs the superior community one delisted. The remaster cannot compete, simple as.
comments.analysis: This comment reflects a common community perception that the official release is inferior, though it is subjective.

comments.author: nubinetwork
comments.text:
> please be nice to Atari

You're not my mom...
comments.analysis: This comment is dismissive and adds little substantive value to the discussion.

comments.author: maCDzP
comments.text: Now with AI I wonder if it’s possible to just let agents build a perfect emulation of the game. It reminds me of fuzzers. You let the agent go loose on the game and it brute forces every possible state. Then recreates the code. It’s very inefficient- but it probably works.
comments.analysis: While creative, the suggestion is speculative and currently impractical given the complexity of perfect emulation through AI fuzzing.
